fact: Santa Anna's army outnumbered the Alamo defenders roughly twenty to one
source: The Alamo is generally described as ~1,500–1,800 Mexican troops in the final assault against ~180–260 defenders. Figures vary by source. See: "A Time to Stand" by Walter Lord (1961) and the Texas State Historical Association.

fact: The siege lasted thirteen days before the final assault
source: Texas State Historical Association, Handbook of Texas — 'Alamo, Battle of the.' The siege ran from February 23 to March 6, 1836 (13 days).

fact: Santa Anna raised a red flag signaling no quarter (no mercy, no surrender accepted)
source: "A Time to Stand" by Walter Lord (1961); also documented in the Alamo's own historical resources. The blood-red flag was raised from San Fernando Church.

fact: Approximately two thousand Mexican soldiers attacked before dawn on March 6, 1836, hitting multiple walls simultaneously
source: Estimates of the assault force range from ~1,500 to ~2,000. Four columns attacked simultaneously. See Timothy Todish, "Alamo Sourcebook 1836" (1998) and the Texas State Historical Association.

fact: All roughly two hundred defenders were killed, and the battle lasted about ninety minutes
source: Defender counts range from ~180–257 depending on the source. The final assault lasted roughly 60–90 minutes. See the Texas General Land Office and TSHA Handbook of Texas.

fact: One general thirty miles away (Sam Houston or James Fannin) decided not to send reinforcements to the Alamo
source: James Fannin at Goliad (~95 miles away) attempted and aborted a relief march; Sam Houston was farther away and did not order relief. The '30 miles' figure is not standard — Fannin was at Goliad, roughly 95 miles southeast. This claim appears to be an oversimplification. See "Exodus from the Alamo" by Phillip Thomas Tucker (2009) and TSHA Handbook.
